completeness.rationale: Captured 5 of 7 expected items: task count (~5,000), title-vs-description distinction, v2.2→v2.3 regression, timing (~2-3 weeks), and 10-15s latency. Missing: CPU spikes during search and long descriptions in tasks (some 2000+ words). Both omissions are relevant to understanding why the regression is so severe.

accuracy.rationale: The root cause hypothesis is essentially correct and matches both acceptable diagnostic paths simultaneously — it identifies the v2.3 upgrade as the trigger AND the title-vs-description indexing difference. Crucially, it explicitly names 'a change from indexed full-text search to unindexed LIKE/ILIKE queries' as the likely cause, which is the actual cause. This is a remarkably accurate hypothesis from limited information.

thoroughness.rationale: Resolved after 2 rounds of questioning. Had a reasonable basis to resolve, but missed obvious follow-ups: CPU behavior during search (would have revealed the single-core spike), description length/content (relevant to why LIKE is so expensive), and whether any cache clearing or reindexing had been attempted. These were not obscure questions — CPU behavior is a natural next step when investigating a performance regression.

economy.rationale: Both rounds of questions were well-targeted and non-redundant. First round covered reproduction context and scale; second covered environment and version. No wasted turns, no repeated asks.

question_quality.rationale: The first question's framing ('does it happen with every search query or only certain ones') was open enough to surface the title-vs-description distinction that the reporter wouldn't have volunteered. The agent then explicitly flagged that distinction as a diagnostic clue and pivoted to version information — demonstrating real reasoning. Questions were professional and well-sequenced. Slight deduction because the second round was fairly standard environmental gathering.

actionability.rationale: The summary is exceptionally actionable: it includes reproduction steps seeded with the right dataset size, a specific recommended fix (compare query paths, run EXPLAIN ANALYZE, check migration scripts), a proposed regression test, and clear information gaps. A developer could begin investigating and writing a fix immediately.

notable_strengths: Correctly identified the title-vs-description performance asymmetry as the key diagnostic clue and called it out explicitly
Root cause hypothesis named 'indexed full-text search to unindexed LIKE/ILIKE queries' — essentially the actual cause — from only two rounds of questioning
Exceptionally detailed, developer-ready summary with reproduction steps, recommended fix approach, and a proposed regression test case
notable_weaknesses: Never asked about CPU behavior during searches — a natural performance question that would have confirmed the single-core spike
Never asked about the nature or length of task descriptions — relevant to why a LIKE scan is so costly at 5,000 rows
Acknowledged uncertainty about upgrade timing in information_gaps but did not press the reporter to check upgrade logs or app version history to confirm
most_insightful_question: The first question's inclusion of 'does it happen with every search query or only certain ones (e.g., longer queries, specific filters)' — this open framing prompted the reporter to volunteer the title-vs-description distinction, which became the central diagnostic clue.
missed_opportunities: Ask whether CPU usage spikes during slow searches (would reveal the single-core saturation pattern)
Ask whether task descriptions tend to be long or contain large amounts of text (would surface the meeting-notes detail relevant to LIKE scan cost)
Ask whether the reporter had tried any workarounds such as cache clearing or reindexing
